text.title: XP Antipatterns
text.summary: Production code before tests, speculative generality, hero culture, no customer access, sustainable pace violation, and agent-specific failures.

antipattern.description: Writing production code before the failing test exists.
antipattern.reason: Tests written after the fact verify the implementation, not the intended behavior. Pre-existing code biases test authors to test what was built rather than what was required. Invert the order unconditionally.

antipattern.description: Agent adds interfaces, strategies, or factory classes without 2+ concrete implementations.
antipattern.reason: Speculative generality violates rule 4 (fewest elements). An interface with one implementation is a naming exercise that adds indirection without enabling extension. Reject any abstraction until the second concrete use case exists.

antipattern.description: Combining refactor and feature changes in one commit.
antipattern.reason: Reviewers cannot distinguish structural change from behavioral change in a mixed commit. Refactors that inadvertently change behavior are invisible. Separate commits enable independent review of each concern.

antipattern.description: Hero culture: one person owns a module and others cannot touch it.
antipattern.reason: Single-owner modules become bottlenecks and bus-factor risks. XP's collective ownership requires that any developer (or agent) can modify any code — enabled by comprehensive tests and standards.

antipattern.description: LLM writes trivial getter/setter tests to hit coverage percentage instead of behavior tests.
antipattern.reason: Coverage percentage does not measure test quality; mutation score does. Trivial tests that call a getter and assert the returned value contribute 0 to mutation score and give false confidence.

antipattern.description: Agent or human changes the CI test threshold or coding standards file without team review.
antipattern.reason: Standards files and CI thresholds are team-level decisions. An agent that lowers a coverage threshold to make a PR green is removing a quality gate, not fixing a quality problem.

antipattern.description: Sustainable pace violated by consistent late-night or weekend commits.
antipattern.reason: Tired developers write more bugs than they fix. Sprint velocity drops as bug rate rises and retrospectives become complaints sessions. Track commit timestamps; sustained off-hours patterns signal scope reduction is needed, not more hours.
